one_line_takeaway: On tabular phishing-URL and network-intrusion MLP classifiers, FGSM/PGD adversarial perturbations degrade accuracy and destabilize SHAP explanations via the same high-leverage features, and FGSM-based adversarial training raises the paper's proposed Robustness Index by 0.10-0.19 with only a 1-2 point clean-accuracy cost.
key_finding: On the Phishing Websites dataset, clean accuracy of the baseline MLP is 0.91; under FGSM at eps=0.3 accuracy drops to about 0.30, while PGD stays comparatively stable near 0.72 (RI_FGSM=0.615-0.62, RI_PGD=0.72-0.756 depending on table). On UNSW-NB15, baseline clean accuracy is 0.74, with smoother degradation (RI_FGSM=0.692, RI_PGD=0.733). Counterintuitively, PGD (a theoretically stronger iterative attack) produced consistently higher RI (less damage) than single-step FGSM in both domains; the paper attributes this to the interaction between L-infinity projection, z-score-normalized features, and PGD's smaller incremental step size, which can yield a more conservative effective perturbation than FGSM's full-magnitude single step. Gradient-based feature sensitivity and SHAP attribution drift analyses identified overlapping high-vulnerability features in the Phishing dataset -- sensitivity analysis flagged InsecureForms, NumDash, and FrequentDomainNameMismatch, while SHAP drift flagged PctExtNullSelfRedirectHyperlinksRT and PctExtResourceUrls -- supporting the paper's central claim that adversarial robustness loss and explainability (SHAP) instability are coupled and concentrated in the same high-leverage features. Adversarial training (FGSM-augmented, eps=0.05) improved RI_FGSM from 0.61 to 0.71 (+0.10) and RI_PGD from 0.72 to 0.87 (+0.15) on Phishing, and RI_FGSM from 0.67 to 0.78 (+0.11) and RI_PGD from 0.73 to 0.92 (+0.19) on UNSW-NB15, while clean accuracy dropped only slightly (Phishing 0.91->0.89; UNSW-NB15 0.74->0.73). An appendix table also reports precision/recall/AUC under FGSM at eps=0.1: Phishing (Acc 0.73, Prec 0.74, Rec 0.71, AUC 0.80) and UNSW-NB15 (Acc 0.73, Prec 0.70, Rec 0.68, AUC 0.78).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (distinct from the cited FGSM/PGD/SHAP literature in Related Work, which it explicitly frames as prior work it builds on) is: (1) a unified empirical framework applying L-infinity-bounded FGSM and PGD adversarial perturbations to two structured, tabular cybersecurity classifiers (phishing URL detection and network intrusion detection) rather than image benchmarks; (2) a new scalar metric, the Robustness Index (RI), defined as the normalized area under the accuracy-vs-perturbation curve (RI = (1/eps_max) * integral of Acc(eps) over [0, eps_max]), including a first-order Taylor-expansion derivation in the appendix showing RI approximates clean accuracy plus a term proportional to the initial decay rate of accuracy; (3) a joint analysis coupling gradient-based feature sensitivity (mean absolute gradient of loss w.r.t. each feature) with SHAP attribution drift (mean absolute change in SHAP values between clean and adversarial inputs) to show the same high-leverage features drive both classification confidence and adversarial/explainability instability; and (4) an adversarial-training ablation (FGSM-augmented training, eps=0.05, 20% of each batch) measuring RI gains at the cost of small clean-accuracy drops.
threat_model: White-box, gradient-access adversary who can apply small Lp-bounded (specifically L-infinity, budget epsilon in [0, 0.3]) perturbations directly to the numerical/engineered feature vectors of a trained MLP classifier (two hidden layers, 64 and 32 neurons, ReLU) -- not to raw URLs or raw packets. The paper explicitly discusses 'Attack Realism for Structured Features,' acknowledging that not all engineered features (e.g., URL structure ratios, flow-level statistics) are independently manipulable by a real attacker, and frames its threat model as 'a realistic upper bound on adversarial capability' intended for worst-case stress-testing of model robustness rather than a claim of practical exploitability. Attacks considered: single-step FGSM and multi-step PGD (alpha=0.01, 10 iterations), both under an L-infinity constraint.
limitations: The paper itself states: (1) it focuses only on differentiable neural classifiers to enable gradient-based sensitivity analysis; evaluating robustness/explainability drift for non-differentiable models (e.g., tree-based classifiers, which are common in production security tooling) is left to future work. (2) The threat model perturbs engineered/aggregate features directly, and the paper concedes not all such features are independently manipulable by a real-world attacker (e.g., raw URL text or packet bytes are not directly perturbed), limiting real-world attack realism despite the paper's framing of this as an intentional 'upper bound' stress test. (3) Only a single architecture (a small 2-hidden-layer MLP) and only two datasets were evaluated; no comparison to other model families (e.g., gradient-boosted trees, CNNs on raw features) is provided. (4) Certified defenses and stronger optimization-based attacks (e.g., Carlini-Wagner) mentioned in Related Work are not evaluated empirically by this paper -- they are explicitly deferred to future work. (5) Code/notebooks are stated as available only 'upon reasonable request,' not published, limiting independent reproducibility verification.

datasets_used: Phishing Websites dataset (Kaggle, Work 2018): 11,000 labeled URL samples, 30 engineered numerical features, binary phishing/legitimate label
UNSW-NB15 dataset (Moustafa and Slay 2015; Kaggle mirror by Wells 2019): 175,000 network traffic records, 42 flow-level/protocol features, binary normal/malicious label
quantitative_results: Phishing RI_FGSM (baseline): 0.61-0.62 (reported as 0.615 in Table 2, 0.61-0.62 in text)
Phishing RI_PGD (baseline): 0.72-0.756 (reported differently in Table 1 vs Table 2)
UNSW-NB15 RI_FGSM (baseline): 0.692 (also stated as 0.67 in Table 1)
After adversarial training (eps=0.05, 20% batch augmentation): Phishing clean acc 0.91->0.89, RI_FGSM 0.61->0.71 (+0.10), RI_PGD 0.72->0.87 (+0.15)
After adversarial training: UNSW-NB15 clean acc 0.74->0.73, RI_FGSM 0.67->0.78 (+0.11), RI_PGD 0.73->0.92 (+0.19)
Appendix Table 3 (FGSM, eps=0.1): Phishing Acc 0.73, Precision 0.74, Recall 0.71, AUC 0.80; UNSW-NB15 Acc 0.73, Precision 0.70, Recall 0.68, AUC 0.78
SHAP drift and gradient sensitivity computed on 256 random test samples per dataset
baselines_compared: Own baseline (non-adversarially-trained) MLP vs. own FGSM-adversarially-trained MLP, per dataset -- this is an internal ablation, not a comparison to external prior defense methods
FGSM attack vs. PGD attack (both authors' own implementation) compared against each other on the same models

No external published defense/detection baselines (e.g., certified defenses of Cohen et al. or Zhang et al., cited in Related Work) were empirically run by this paper; those are cited as prior work only
